messages.content:
YOUR ROLE: You are the partner running a pre-mortem on this market model for a
paying client. The client's own team has already read every number in the
ledger, including every sensitivity band. Your reputation rests on surfacing
what THEY would not list: risks that look benign on the surface but could kill
the venture, argued mechanistically, anchored in the model.

HARD REQUIREMENTS — a hypothesis that misses any of these will be rejected by
a validator before any human reads it:
1. targetNodes: real ledger node ids; perturbation ops only on FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes.
2. mechanism: at least 3 steps (trigger → propagation → model impact), EVERY
   step carrying refs — "ledger:<nodeId>", "digest:<group>#c<n>", or
   "profile:<field>". A step you cannot reference is a step you are guessing.
3. falsifier: the single concrete observation that would kill this risk. If you
   cannot name one, it is not a claim.
4. whyMissable: why a competent founder's own risk list omits this. "It's
   non-obvious" is not an argument; name the surface appearance that hides it.
5. likelihood: a probability with a basis ("evidence" | "base-rate" |
   "judgment") and a rationale. No naked numbers.
6. indicators: at least 2 observable early warnings, each with WHERE to watch
   (a named tender portal, an operator's capex call, a standards docket, a
   vendor's price list) and the reading that should trigger re-assessment.
7. mitigations: at least 2. Where the right move is to buy information, use
   type "information" and name the ledger node it de-risks (voiNodeId) — its
   value will be computed, not asserted.

QUOTAS (per lens): at least one hypothesis self-tagged expectedObviousness
"low", and at least one whose perturbation targets NONE of tamBase /
serviceableFactor / obtainableFactor — the tornado already covers those bands;
look past them.

MINE THE RESIDUALS: the highest-yield material is where sources disagree with
the curated value (the digests' "tensions"), where searches came back empty
(the digests' "absences" — an unquantified market is a risk, not a comfort),
where a derivation's crossCheck diverges from its primary method, and where a
precise-looking value carries maturity "needs-source".

HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

BANNED GENRES (emitting one of these wastes your slot):
- "Find 2 customers to talk to and get an LOI" — an action item, not a risk. It names no node, no mechanism, no falsifier.
- "Do research on customer buying behaviour" — a homework assignment any consultant could write for any company.
- "Competition is strong / the market is concentrated" — the ledger already states CR3. Restating a fact is not a risk; the risk is a MECHANISM that concentration enables.
- "The market may be smaller than expected" — the tamBase band (240–520) already says this. Only worth saying if you name WHICH derivation hop fails and WHY.
- "Regulatory changes could impact the business" — name the instrument, the compliance artifact it requires, and the date it lands, or drop it.
- "The band's low end might be true" with no new reason — the band is already in the model; you are paid for what is NOT in the model.

GOLD EXEMPLAR (the target register — do NOT repeat its content):
{
 "id": "busway-hollowing-hyperscale",
 "title": "Busway + OCP power shelves hollow out the hyperscale 44% from inside the scope boundary",
 "narrative": "The model excludes busbars and power shelves as out-of-scope substitutes — a scoping footnote. But hyperscale is 44% of TAM, and it is precisely the segment whose new AI-density builds are moving power distribution from rack PDUs to overhead busway feeding OCP-style in-rack power shelves. If that architecture becomes the default for new CE hyperscale capacity, the largest slice of this TAM is not 'ours to win slowly' — it is structurally shrinking while the headline number stays flat. The exclusion list quietly became the market thesis.",
 "category": "boundary",
 "targetNodes": [
  "seg.hyperscale"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "New hyperscale AI-density builds adopt busway + in-rack DC power shelves instead of discrete rack PDUs",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c?",
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "CE hyperscale capacity additions (the growth the 9% CAGR is made of) carry falling rack-PDU content per MW; replacement demand in legacy halls does not offset it",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:shape.cagr",
    "digest:tam-base#c?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "The hyperscale share of the rack-PDU market shrinks in absolute terms — seg.hyperscale 0.44 overstates the addressable slice for a new entrant's window",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "Substitutes were explicitly considered and excluded — the boundary decision looks handled. The kill path is not 'we misjudged the boundary' but 'the boundary moved into the biggest segment while the TAM base year stayed put'.",
 "expectedObviousness": "low",
 "falsifier": "2025–26 CE hyperscale fit-out specs (or Schneider/Vertiv rack-power order mixes) showing intelligent rack PDUs holding share in new AI-density halls.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.35,
  "rationale": "OCP power-shelf adoption is documented for AI racks, but CE colocation and enterprise retrofits (56% of TAM) keep buying rack PDUs; timing within a 12-month window is uncertain.",
  "basis": "evidence"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "seg.hyperscale",
   "op": "set",
   "value": 0.22,
   "note": "≈half of the hyperscale slice migrates to busway/shelf architectures"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Rack-power architecture specified in new CE hyperscale tenders (busway/shelf vs PDU)",
   "where": "Frankfurt/Amsterdam data-center fit-out tenders; OCP regional summit deployment talks",
   "threshold": "≥2 flagship CE builds specifying shelf-based distribution",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "Incumbent rack-PDU revenue mix commentary (Vertiv/Schneider earnings)",
   "where": "Quarterly earnings calls, rack-power product-line commentary",
   "threshold": "PDU line flat while busway line grows double-digit",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Commission a CE rack-power architecture survey across the 2026 hyperscale build pipeline",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "seg.hyperscale"
  },
  {
   "action": "Qualify the product line for colocation/enterprise retrofit first, where PDU form-factor is sticky",
   "type": "strategic"
  }
 ]
}

content: {"hypotheses":[{"id":"europe-share-phantom-multiplier","title":"The 'Europe ≈ 30%' step in the TAM chain is a number nobody published — the whole funnel hangs on an invented multiplier","narrative":"tamBase is derived as Global $2.81B × Europe ≈ 30% × CE 39.8%. The middle factor is the load-bearing one, and the cited GVR source states only North America's 38% share — no source in the corpus states Europe's rack-PDU share at all. With NA at 38% and APAC absorbing the fastest hyperscale buildout, Europe's true share of a rack-PDU market whose growth is capacity-led could plausibly sit at 20–25%. A hostile diligence partner strikes the 30% first because it is the only unattributed factor in an otherwise sourced expression, and every downstream node — SAM, YAM, every geo and segment slice — scales linearly with it.","category":"fact","targetNodes":["tamBase"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The derivation's 'Europe ≈ 30% [est.]' factor is flagged in the corpus as unsourced: GVR publishes only NA = 38%, and no retrieved source states a Europe rack-PDU share","refs":["digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c1","digest:tam-base#c1","ledger:tamBase"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Global publisher estimates for the base itself already span >2x ($1.7B–$3.94B), so an invented regional split compounds on an unstable base; if Europe is 22–25% (consistent with capacity-led demand concentrating in NA/APAC AI builds), the €775M Europe base falls to €570–650M before the CE step is applied","refs":["digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c0","digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c4","ledger:tamBase"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"tamBase re-derives to roughly €230–260M — below the curated €320M and near the band's low edge for a structural reason the band does not encode (the band was framed as source dispersion, not as a phantom multiplier); SAM and YAM scale down proportionally","refs":["ledger:tamBase","ledger:serviceableFactor","ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"The derivation expression looks fully sourced — every other factor carries a named publisher and date, so the '≈30% [est.]' reads as one more citation rather than the single invented number in the chain. The founder's team sees a triangulated-looking formula, not a formula with a hole in the middle.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"A directly sourced Europe rack-PDU revenue share ≥28% — e.g., the IndexBox EU rack-PDU country tables or an Omdia regional vendor-revenue split showing Europe at or above the assumed level.","likelihood":{"value":0.4,"rationale":"The corpus explicitly confirms no source states Europe's share, and NA=38% plus documented APAC/NA AI-capacity concentration makes a sub-30% Europe share at least as likely as 30%+; but Europe's dense colo base could also support the assumed level","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"tamBase","op":"set","value":235,"note":"Europe share re-set to ~23% of global instead of 30%; CE step unchanged"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"EMEA share of rack-power / critical-infrastructure segment revenue disclosed by Vertiv and Schneider","where":"Vertiv 10-K segment disclosures and Schneider Electric annual report, Energy Management segment regional split","threshold":"EMEA consistently <25% of relevant segment revenue","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Headline EU rack-PDU market size once the directly-scoped report is opened","where":"IndexBox 'Rack PDUs Market in the European Union' report (currently paywalled, named in the ledger's promotion path)","threshold":"EU figure implying Europe <27% of the GVR global base","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission the IndexBox EU rack-PDU report (country tables) to replace the invented regional split with a directly-scoped EU base","type":"information","voiNodeId":"tamBase","note":"Named in the ledger's own promotion path; collapses the ±40% band and kills or confirms this hypothesis in one purchase"},{"action":"Rebuild tamBase bottom-up as CE colocation MW × PDU content €/MW, bypassing the global→Europe→CE scope-down entirely","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Uses the Statista MW data already in the ledger plus a per-rack PDU price point; independent of any regional revenue split"}]},{"id":"buyer-mix-fabricated-odm-leakage","title":"The 0.40 'large operators buy direct' share is one misread order-book stat — and the corpus says the real large-operator volume flows through ODM white-label the entrant can't intercept","narrative":"The venture's stated go-to-market is 'large operators first', and the model rewards that with a 0.40 customer-type slice. But the only attached evidence for 0.40 is a company-level statistic (one supplier's DC orders = 30% of its book) that says nothing about buyer channels, and cust.oem/distributor carry zero evidence. Meanwhile the same digest documents the actual structure: hyperscale and large-colo procurement increasingly runs through Taiwanese ODM/white-label programs (Digipower, GETEKnet) and master distributors — spend that is nominally 'large operator' but contractually locked into OEM design-win cycles a pre-launch EU brand cannot enter in Year 1. The largest customer node isn't just uncertain; it is partly not a channel this entrant can address at all.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["cust.operator-large","cust.oem"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"cust.operator-large's 0.40 rests on a single order-mix datapoint (DC = 30% of one supplier's orders) misused as a market channel split; no source publishes any numeric buyer-channel breakdown","refs":["digest:cust-mix#c0","ledger:cust.operator-large"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Documented ODM/white-label supply (Digipower serving 'global OEM partners and infrastructure operators', GETEKnet as explicit OEM rack-PDU supplier, distributors offering private-label) means a material fraction of large-operator PDU volume is procured as white-label design wins with multi-year qualification cycles, not open branded tenders","refs":["digest:cust-mix#c11","digest:cust-mix#c12","digest:cust-mix#c13","digest:cust-mix#c8"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The direct-branded large-operator slice shrinks (0.40→~0.27) while the OEM/integrator slice grows — but grows in a channel where a pre-launch entrant competes on ODM economics and 18–24 month qualification, so the venture's declared primary channel overstates its Year-1 reachable spend","refs":["ledger:cust.operator-large","ledger:cust.oem","profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?"]}],"whyMissable":"The four buyer-mix numbers sum neatly to 1.00 and mirror the capacity mix, so the axis looks internally consistent; and 'sell to large operators' matches the segment story (hyperscale+colo = 62%). Nothing on the surface reveals that the largest cell was extrapolated from a statistic about a different question, or that its volume is contractually routed through a channel with different economics.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"Three structured CE operator/distributor interviews (the node's own promotion path) confirming that ≥35% of CE rack-PDU spend is direct operator purchase of branded units on open tender.","likelihood":{"value":0.45,"rationale":"The evidentiary hole is documented in the digest (zero attached evidence for three of four nodes, one misused stat for the fourth), and ODM white-label supply is qualitatively confirmed by multiple vendors; only the magnitude of the shift is uncertain","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"cust.operator-large","op":"set","value":0.27,"note":"Direct branded large-operator spend rewritten down; part of the former 0.40 is ODM-locked"},{"nodeId":"cust.oem","op":"set","value":0.3,"note":"Mix shift: volume reappears in the OEM/ODM channel — deliberately nets -5pp because some white-label volume is unaddressable to a CE brand entrant at any price"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"PDU specification language in large CE operator RFQs — 'ODM/white-label per our spec' vs open branded procurement","where":"Hyperscaler supplier-registration portals and Frankfurt/Amsterdam colocation fit-out RFQ packages","threshold":"≥2 of first 5 target accounts procuring PDUs exclusively via ODM programs or rack-integrator bundles","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Channel-mix commentary from incumbents — direct vs partner revenue split in EMEA","where":"Vertiv EMEA partner-program materials and quarterly earnings channel commentary; Schneider partner announcements","threshold":"Incumbents reporting growing share of rack-power revenue via integrator/ODM programs","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Rack-level pre-integration announcements (PDUs shipped pre-installed in integrated racks)","where":"OCP summit talks and rack-integrator (system-build) press releases for CE deployments","threshold":"Pre-integrated racks specified in ≥2 flagship CE builds","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Run the three CE operator/distributor interviews already named in the node's promotion path, structured specifically to split direct-branded vs ODM/white-label vs distributor volume","type":"information","voiNodeId":"cust.operator-large","note":"Replaces the misused 30%-of-orders stat with a sourced channel split; also de-risks cust.oem and cust.distributor"},{"action":"Develop an OEM/private-label variant of the product line so ODM-routed large-operator volume becomes addressable rather than foreclosed","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Converts the channel risk into a second route to market at lower margin"},{"action":"Sign a CE master distributor with existing operator framework agreements to cover the mid/enterprise slice while direct large-operator qualification runs","type":"contractual","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Hedges Year-1 revenue against a slow direct channel"}]},{"id":"ce-hyperscale-colo-inversion","title":"Synergy's global 44/18 split, imported wholesale, inverts the actual European ordering — CE is a colocation-led market wearing a hyperscale-led costume","narrative":"seg.hyperscale (0.44) and seg.colocation (0.18) are a global Synergy capacity split rescaled by an unverified 0.91 carve-out and applied to Central Europe. But CBRE's Europe-specific reporting states colocation 'will remain significantly larger' than hyperscaler self-build in Europe — the opposite ordering — and 57% of 2025's new European capacity lands in FLAPD colo markets. No Europe- or CE-scoped hyperscale share has ever been observed; the derivation's own 'cross-check' compares a raw global number against a rescaled estimate and calls the coincidence validation. If CE is actually colo-led, the venture's segment map, certification priorities, and reference-account strategy are calibrated to a market mix that doesn't exist where it is selling.","category":"fact","targetNodes":["seg.hyperscale","seg.colocation"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"CBRE Europe-scoped reporting contradicts the imported global split: colocation remains significantly larger than hyperscale self-build in Europe, versus the model's hyperscale ≈ 2.4× colocation","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c15","digest:seg-mix#c14","ledger:seg.hyperscale"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The segment nodes rest on a single publisher (Synergy, global scope) rescaled by a 0.91 carve-out that no source verifies; the claimed cross-check compares different quantities (raw global 44% vs rescaled CE 0.44), so the numeric agreement is a coincidence, not triangulation — meanwhile European capacity-flow data shows colo-led delivery (57% of 2025 new supply in FLAPD colo markets)","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c0","digest:seg-mix#c19","digest:seg-mix#c6","digest:geo-region-denominator#c8"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The CE segment mix flips: hyperscale slice roughly halves and colocation roughly doubles; the venture's TAM composition, product certification sequence, and named-account targeting — all pointed at hyperscale — address a slice half its modeled size, while the actually-larger colo segment carries a partially different buying structure","refs":["ledger:seg.hyperscale","ledger:seg.colocation","profile:Product"]}],"whyMissable":"seg.hyperscale is one of only two segment nodes carrying maturity 'triangulated' and a sensitivity band — it looks like the best-evidenced cell on the axis. The triangulation is between two vintages of the same publisher's same global series, and the 0.91 rescaling makes the raw and derived numbers coincide at 0.44, manufacturing the appearance of a cross-check.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"A Europe- or CE-scoped capacity split (CBRE, Synergy Europe cut, or EUDCA country tables) showing hyperscale self-build + leased-hyperscale at ≥40% of CE data-center capacity.","likelihood":{"value":0.45,"rationale":"CBRE's Europe-specific claim directly contradicts the model's ordering and is corroborated by FLAPD colo-led delivery data; the residual uncertainty is whether 'scale-colocation' leased to hyperscalers should count toward the hyperscale or colo buyer for PDU purposes","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"seg.hyperscale","op":"set","value":0.25,"note":"CE hyperscale self-build share rewritten from Europe-scoped evidence"},{"nodeId":"seg.colocation","op":"set","value":0.37,"note":"Mix shift toward colocation per CBRE Europe ordering; siblings moved together since no re-normalization occurs"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Colocation take-up vs hyperscale self-build delivery split for European markets","where":"CBRE 'Europe Data Centres' quarterly figures reports; Synergy Research Europe-specific press releases","threshold":"Colo supply/take-up ≥2× identifiable self-build additions in CE markets for two consecutive quarters","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Buyer identity on CE capacity announcements — operator self-build vs colo provider (Equinix, NTT, Data4, Vantage) with hyperscale pre-lease","where":"Data Center Dynamics CE project announcements; Frankfurt/Amsterdam/Warsaw planning filings","threshold":"≥70% of announced CE MW carried by colo providers rather than operator self-build","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Purchase or commission a Europe-scoped capacity split (CBRE bespoke cut or Synergy Europe series) to replace the rescaled global Synergy figure","type":"information","voiNodeId":"seg.hyperscale","note":"Named in the node's own promotion path; a single Europe-scoped datapoint resolves the ordering"},{"action":"Sequence go-to-market to lead with CE colocation providers (where the mix flip would enlarge the target) and treat hyperscale as a Year-2 design-win track","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Makes the venture robust to either ordering rather than betting the ramp on the global split being right"}]},{"id":"serviceable-factor-conflation","title":"The 0.55 serviceable factor conflates 'segments we'd like' with 'compliance-cleared demand' — the corpus puts the reachable slice below the band's floor","narrative":"serviceableFactor 0.55 is the model's only pure assumption leaf on the SAM path, justified by three EU cert regimes cited qualitatively. The corpus disagrees in two directions at once: every quantified B2B analogue found puts SAM far below 45–65% of TAM (worked examples near 12%), and the search surfaced a materially larger regulatory stack than the node cites — WEEE, RoHS, EORI customs registration, the 2024/1364 DC rating scheme, and EED 2024/1791 KPI/waste-heat disclosure. Each omitted instrument is a compliance artifact with lead time that gates tender prequalification. For a pre-launch entrant in its first 12 months, the demand that is actually open to bid is not 55% of TAM; it is the fraction whose tenders it can prequalify for before certifications complete — plausibly below 0.40, under the band's floor for a reason the band never encoded.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["serviceableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The 0.55 is unanchored: the only quantified analogues in the corpus are SAM examples near 12% of TAM and SOM benchmarks of 1–10%, all far below the 0.45–0.65 band; simultaneously the cited cert basis (EN 50600, 2019/424, CE/TÜV) omits WEEE, RoHS, EORI, 2024/1364, and EED 2024/1791","refs":["digest:serviceable-benchmark#c11","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c13","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c8","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c9","ledger:serviceableFactor"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Each omitted instrument lands as a procurement gate: EED KPI and waste-heat disclosure obligations and the 2024/1364 rating scheme flow into operator tender prequalification, so demand from compliance-sensitive large operators (the venture's declared first channel) is closed to an entrant mid-certification during exactly its first 12 months","refs":["digest:serviceable-benchmark#c9","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c10","profile:Stage","profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The serviceable share of TAM for the Year-1 window sits below the 0.45 floor; SAM contracts beyond anything the tornado shows, and YAM contracts with it even at the curated 3% obtainable rate","refs":["ledger:serviceableFactor","ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"The node already carries a wide band (0.45–0.65) and cites real EU regulations, so it reads as a conservatively handled assumption. The hidden failure is definitional: 0.55 was set as 'share of segments we intend to serve', while the funnel needs 'share of demand whose tenders we can prequalify for in the first 12 months' — two different quantities that happen to share a slider.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"A bottom-up channel-coverage model (named CE accounts × compliance-cleared demand, the node's own promotion path) yielding a serviceable share ≥0.50 for the Year-1 window.","likelihood":{"value":0.4,"rationale":"The corpus documents both the missing regulatory instruments and the absence of any benchmark supporting 0.45–0.65; but part of the gap may be definitional slack rather than a true foreclosure, and mid-market buyers are less prequalification-heavy","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"serviceableFactor","op":"set","value":0.38,"note":"Year-1 compliance-cleared, channel-reachable share; below the band floor per corpus benchmarks and the expanded cert stack"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Certification and disclosure requirements appearing in CE operator PDU/rack-power tender prequalification lists","where":"Frankfurt/Amsterdam/Warsaw colocation procurement packages; hyperscaler supplier-onboarding portals","threshold":"EED 2024/1791 KPI or 2024/1364 rating-scheme references in ≥2 of first 5 target-account tenders","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Implementation timeline and scope of the EU DC rating scheme and EED delegated acts","where":"EUR-Lex / European Commission energy-efficiency dockets for Directive 2024/1791 and Delegated Regulation 2024/1364","threshold":"Supplier-facing obligations confirmed to apply at equipment-procurement level within 12 months","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Own certification program milestones vs plan (CE, TÜV, RoHS/WEEE registration, EORI)","where":"Internal cert tracker vs notified-body lead-time quotes","threshold":"Any critical cert slipping past month 6 of the launch window","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Build the bottom-up channel-coverage model named in the node's promotion path: list the top 50 CE accounts, map each one's prequalification requirements, and compute the compliance-cleared share directly","type":"information","voiNodeId":"serviceableFactor","note":"Replaces the conflated slider with two separate, measurable quantities"},{"action":"Front-load the full certification stack (including WEEE/RoHS/EORI, not just CE/TÜV) before launch rather than sequencing it with revenue","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Converts the Year-1 foreclosure into a cost line"},{"action":"Route early sales through a distributor/integrator that already holds operator framework qualifications, inheriting their compliance clearance","type":"contractual","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Buys reach into prequalification-gated demand during the certification gap"}]},{"id":"statista-scale-colo-scope-inflation","title":"One vendor's unverified 'colocation and scale-colocation' definition is both the geo split and a hidden multiplier inside tamBase — normalization launders the scope error out of sight","narrative":"Every geo node and the 39.8% CE-of-Europe step inside tamBase come from a single Statista series whose scope no second vendor reproduces. The corpus shows the series runs hot: summed 2024 Statista country colo (~7.6GW) exceeds CBRE's 5.8GW figure for ALL European DC capacity, and PMR's CEE numbers imply a regional base ~5× smaller than the Statista-derived one. Because the seven geo shares are normalized to 1.00, the axis looks self-consistent no matter how wrong the underlying series is — but the same series also sets the absolute CE-of-Europe fraction in tamBase, where a scope inflation does not cancel. If 'scale-colocation' quietly includes hyperscale self-build shells or contracted-not-energized capacity, the CE market is being sized off MW that either doesn't exist yet or buys PDUs through channels outside this model.","category":"fact","targetNodes":["tamBase"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Statista's per-country colocation figures share no verified common scope with any benchmark: the summed 2024 country values (~7.6GW) exceed CBRE's 4,726MW European colo supply and even its 5.8GW total-European-capacity figure, and no second vendor uses the 'colocation and scale-colocation IT power' definition","refs":["digest:geo-region-denominator#c1","digest:geo-region-denominator#c3","digest:geo-region-denominator#c5"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Independent regional checks confirm the same direction of error: PMR/Atman put Poland at ~200MW commercial and >1/3 of a CEE total ≈600MW, versus the Statista-derived CE-7 base of 3,451MW — meaning the series most plausibly counts capacity classes (self-build shells, contracted pipeline) that do not purchase rack PDUs as modeled","refs":["digest:geo-PL#c13","digest:geo-PL#c14","ledger:geo.PL"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The CE-7 = 39.8%-of-Europe step in tamBase's derivation was computed on an inflated numerator and denominator of unknown relative bias; recomputing CE's weight on a CBRE-consistent scope shrinks the CE slice of the European base, cutting tamBase below its curated value independent of the global-base and Europe-share disputes — while the normalized geo shares hide that anything moved","refs":["ledger:tamBase","ledger:geo.DE","digest:geo-region-denominator#c5"]}],"whyMissable":"Each geo node individually shows a cross-check (BMWK, DDCA, CBRE Suisse, Mordor) and several carry 'triangulated' maturity, so the axis reads as well-verified.
